talk_title: Overview of Pediatric Extrapolation in Oncology Drug Development

speaker.name: Lauren Price
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Clinical Pharmacology, Solid Tumor Division

MOA_summary: Pediatric extrapolation leverages similarity in disease biology, drug pharmacology, and response between adult (or older pediatric) reference populations and younger pediatric patients to support safe and efficient drug development without repeating full efficacy trials.
key_findings: More than 50% of new pediatric oncology indications (2013‑2024) used pediatric extrapolation.
ICH E11A (2024) formalizes guidance, including allowance for safety extrapolation when supported by data.
Three‑step framework: concept development, plan creation, and plan execution with iterative updates.
Early FDA engagement (early advice meetings, pediatric study plans) is encouraged to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Model‑Informed Drug Development (MIDD) to Support Pediatric Extrapolation

speaker.name: Jiang Liu
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Deputy Division Director of Pharmacometrics/Quantitative Clinical Pharmacology

MOA_summary: MIDD integrates PK/PD, PBPK, population PK, and QSP models to bridge adult and pediatric data, enabling exposure matching, dose optimization, and trial design for pediatric oncology drugs.
key_findings: MIDD methods (population PK, PBPK, QSP) can predict pediatric exposure and support dose selection without extensive pediatric trials.
Examples: gadolinium‑based contrast agents, RILACE, itolizumab, rituximab—all received pediatric indications down to neonates via MIDD.
Renal maturation modeling shows GFR normalizes after ~2 years, informing GBCA dosing in infants.
FDA recommends early, iterative MIDD discussions (type D, MIDD‑focused meetings) to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Lutathera in Adolescents with SSTR‑Positive GEP‑NETs

speaker.name: Nicole Dresner
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Deputy Director, Division of Oncology 2

biomarkers.name: SSTR expression
biomarkers.finding: Present in >90% of adolescent GEP‑NETs, supporting target relevance

models.name: NETTER‑P
models.genotype_or_key_features: Adolescents with SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs or PPGLs, n=9 (4 GEP‑NET)

MOA_summary: Lutathera delivers beta‑emitting 177Lu to SSTR‑expressing neuroendocrine tumor cells, causing DNA damage and tumor regression; adult NETTER‑1 showed a 79% reduction in progression risk (HR 0.21) and 13% ORR, supporting extrapolation to adolescents with similar SSTR expression.
key_findings: Adult NETTER‑1 trial demonstrated PFS benefit (HR 0.21) and ORR 13% for Lutathera in SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs.
NETTER‑P enrolled 9 adolescents; safety profile matched adults (lymphopenia, neutropenia).
Organ dosimetry (kidney, bone marrow) in adolescents was comparable to adults, though predicted high‑dose kidney probability exceeded 20% at the 95th percentile.
FDA concluded extrapolation appropriate despite limited pediatric efficacy data, issuing PMRs for long‑term renal and hematologic safety.
Lutathera received FDA approval for adolescents with SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs in 2024, marking a successful pediatric extrapolation case.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Blinatumomab in B‑ALL

speaker.name: Mansi Dalal
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Clinical Reviewer, Division of Hemomalignancies 1

biomarkers.name: CD19 expression
biomarkers.finding: Uniformly present in adult and pediatric B‑ALL blasts

biomarkers.name: MRD negativity
biomarkers.finding: 81% of adult BLAST patients achieved MRD‑negative status after one cycle

models.name: MT103205
models.genotype_or_key_features: Children <18 y, relapsed/refractory Ph‑negative B‑ALL, n≈30

MOA_summary: Blinatumomab is a CD19‑directed CD3 bispecific T‑cell engager that forms an immunologic synapse, activating T‑cells to lyse CD19‑positive B‑ALL blasts; PK exposure matching between adults and children supports similar efficacy and safety extrapolation.
key_findings: Adult BLAST study showed 81% MRD‑negative conversion, providing a robust efficacy signal for extrapolation.
Pediatric dose‑finding study (MT103205) demonstrated tolerability and similar cytokine release syndrome rates to adults.

Meta‑analysis of three randomized trials (adult E1910, pediatric 201/20215, AALL 1331) plus BLIM‑FANT showed consistent survival benefit across ages, supporting extrapolation to pediatric consolidation therapy.
FDA granted age‑agnostic indications for blinatumomab in MRD‑positive B‑ALL and in consolidation, relying on adult efficacy and pediatric safety/PK data.
Post‑marketing requirements include long‑term safety follow‑up for neurotoxicity and cytokine release syndrome.

combination_strategies.rationale: Intercalating blinatumomab improves survival when combined with multi‑phase chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Consistent OS hazard ratio benefit across adult and pediatric trials
